      Uninvestable
   =20
 =20
Sometimes a financial asset trades for $100 one day and $5 the next. If you=
 own it, you have a 95% mark-to-market loss. You might, for various reasons=
, have to sell it quickly at a loss. Perhaps you are a mutual fund and inve=
stors in your fund all want to redeem because you lost 95% of their money, =
so you are forced to sell the asset to raise cash. Or perhaps you are a lev=
ered investor:=C2=A0a hedge fund, or just a person with a margin loan. You =
paid for the asset with $50 of your own money and $50 borrowed from the ban=
k, and now it is worth $5. The bank calls you up and says =E2=80=9Chey=C2=
=A0you borrowed $50 against something now worth $5, so would you mind payin=
g us back $45?=E2=80=9D[1] And you politely decline, because (1) why give t=
he bank $45 to keep something worth $5 and (2) given the circumstances you =
will probably have a hard time raising the $45. So the bank=C2=A0seizes the=
 asset, sells it for $5, keeps the $5=C2=A0and has a $45 loss on its loan.T=
his is bad, but it is very much stuff that everyone understands and prepare=
s for. You might get forced out of a trade at a loss, the bank might lose m=
oney on margin loans, it=E2=80=99s normal stuff. The bigger, stranger probl=
em is when a financial asset trades at $100 one day and=C2=A0doesn=E2=80=99=
t trade=C2=A0the next. One day willing sellers sell it to willing buyers fo=
r $100; the next day lots of people are desperate to sell but no one can bu=
y. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99ll sell it for $5, we don=E2=80=99t care, we just wa=
nt out,=E2=80=9D the sellers say, but the buyers can=E2=80=99t buy. Maybe t=
he stock exchange has closed. Maybe it has become illegal to transact in th=
e asset. Perhaps there is a little trading =E2=80=94=C2=A0perhaps some buye=
rs in some venue can buy the asset at distressed prices even as most buyers=
 can=E2=80=99t =E2=80=94=C2=A0but not enough for all the sellers to sell. =
=E2=80=9CThis asset was worth $100 yesterday and is worth $5 today,=E2=80=
=9D you can say, fine, but the problem is different. When investors come to=
 your fund looking to redeem, you can=E2=80=99t just get $5 in cash to hand=
 back to them, because you can=E2=80=99t sell. When the bank forecloses on =
the margin loan, it can=E2=80=99t sell to get some cash back; it is stuck h=
olding the thing indefinitely.I don=E2=80=99t know!MSCI Inc. and FTSE Russe=
ll are cutting Russian equities from widely-tracked indexes, while the Lond=
on Stock Exchange suspends dozens of Russian depositary receipts from tradi=
ng, isolating the stocks from a large segment of the investment-fund indust=
ry.An overwhelming majority of market participants see the Russian market a=
s =E2=80=9Cuninvestable=E2=80=9D and its securities will be removed from em=
erging markets indexes effective March 9, MSCI said. FTSE Russell will dele=
te Russia constituents listed on the Moscow Exchange at a zero value on Mar=
ch 7. Meanwhile, trading in 28 depositary receipts for Russian companies ha=
ve been suspended on the LSE, Chief Executive Officer David Schwimmer said =
in an interview with Bloomberg Television on Thursday. =E2=80=A6=E2=80=9CWe=
 can=E2=80=99t sell our Russian stocks,=E2=80=9D said Russel Chesler, head =
of investments and capital markets at fund manager VanEck Associates Corp. =
in Sydney. =C2=A0=E2=80=9CEven last week our brokers wouldn=E2=80=99t sell =
them when the markets were open, and this will just deteriorate things furt=
her for investors.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0The London Stock Exchange=E2=80=99s move t=
o suspend Russian depositary receipts is =E2=80=9Cin connection with events=
 in Ukraine, in light of market conditions, and in order to maintain orderl=
y markets,=E2=80=9D LSE said in a statement. It also noted that less than 1=
% of its total income comes from operations in Russia and Ukraine.On the on=
e hand, if big Russian stocks have lost 95% to 99% of their value, presumab=
ly levered global investors are getting margin calls. On the other hand, if=
 you are a levered global investor, you can=E2=80=99t sell your Russian sto=
cks in Russia (the exchange is closed and brokers have been told not to han=
dle sales for international investors) or abroad (the LSE has suspended tra=
ding in the depository receipts), and your bank probably can=E2=80=99t eith=
er. The whole system is a bit frozen.Or: On the one hand, if you are an eme=
rging-markets index fund and big Russian stocks have been removed from your=
 index for being =E2=80=9Cuninvestable,=E2=80=9D presumably you have to go =
sell those stocks to continue tracking the index. On the other hand,  you c=
an=E2=80=99t:Now, many passive vehicles can=E2=80=99t actually sell to comp=
ly with the index changes, handle investors exiting, or both.=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9CFor ETFs such as broad global ETFs or even broad EM ETFs where the Russi=
a holding is limited, the ETF I guess will continue to hold the Russian ass=
ets, tracking a benchmark where these are removed,=E2=80=9D said Matthew Br=
ennan, head of investment management at AJ Bell.=C2=A0The idea is that the =
Russian securities would continue to be held even if valued at zero. This c=
ould cause tracking error -- when a fund=E2=80=99s actual performance diver=
ges from that of the benchmark it follows -- but also has potential upside =
should the assets ever recover.Or here is a weird technical point:Some U.S.=
 money managers with Russian securities held in funds that focus on emergin=
g markets are worried that their investors will pull money over concern abo=
ut their Russian exposure. If they can=E2=80=99t sell Russian shares to mee=
t the demand, funds could be forced to deplete cash on hand or sell other a=
ssets.That could deliver a double whammy for emerging-market managers: a re=
duction in the share of their funds in liquid assets that coincides with an=
 increase in the weightings of their Russian holdings.Some firms are thinki=
ng about asking the Securities and Exchange Commission for relief from a ca=
p on the proportion of illiquid securities that funds can hold=E2=80=94curr=
ently 15%=E2=80=94and from rules governing redemptions, people familiar wit=
h the matter said. An SEC spokesman declined to comment.If you are a mutual=
 fund manager you are in the business of holding mainly liquid publicly tra=
ded securities. This is important, because you are also in the business of =
letting your investors withdraw their money on a daily basis, so if they wa=
nt their money back, you have to sell liquid securities to give it to them.=
 But when some of your liquid publicly traded securities become illiquid an=
d untraded, then (1) that=E2=80=99s bad and (2) you=E2=80=99re going to hav=
e to sell your=C2=A0other, still liquid securities to meet redemptions, mak=
ing your remaining fund both more illiquid and more concentrated in the Rus=
sian stocks that have now been kicked out of the index.I=E2=80=99m not sure=

Last week, one of the most discussed forms of sanction on Russia for its in=
vasion of Ukraine was the idea of cutting Russian banks off of the SWIFT in=
terbank messaging system. This was widely considered to be a very big deal =
because=C2=A0SWIFT is how banks mainly send payment instructions, and witho=
ut access to SWIFT it will be somewhere between administratively annoying a=
nd almost impossible=C2=A0for Russian banks to interact with the global fin=
ancial system. And this week it sort of happened, on a smallish scale and w=
ith lot of carve-outs.I think the best and deepest explanation of the SWIFT=
 situation is this post from payments expert Patrick McKenzie. It includes =
a discussion of the mechanics of SWIFT and cross-border payments, but that =
is not really the emphasis. Instead the emphasis is on symbolism: Partially=
 cutting some Russian banks off of the SWIFT system =E2=80=9Cdoes not subst=
antially control who is allowed to send wires,=E2=80=9D but it does send a =
certain sort of message to the banks who interact with those Russian banks.=
 McKenzie:I think the best articulation of the strategy is =E2=80=9CWe are =
attempting to convey enormous displeasure while sanctioning some banks whic=
h are believed to be close to politically exposed Russians, while not makin=
g it impossible for Russian firms generally to transact internationally nor=
 sparking a humanitarian crisis either inside or outside of Russia.=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=A6Many commentators confuse the actual effects of severing parti=
cular banks from SWIFT with what they perceive as the policy goal motivatin=
g it. More important than either is, in my opinion, what it communicates ab=
out commander=E2=80=99s intent to the policy arms who are responsible for e=
nforcing it.Specifically, it communicates that Something Has Changed and th=
at Russian institutional money, specifically =E2=80=9Coligarch=E2=80=9D mon=
ey, is now tainted, and not in the benignly ignored fashion it has been for=
 most of the last few decades.Read: =E2=80=9CWe will with absolute certaint=
y hand out billions of dollars of fines stochastically over the next ten ye=
ars. You can minimize how many hit your institution by successfully intuiti=
ng who is on the Bad Risks list. We will be sharply less tolerant of =E2=80=
=98Cyprus is an EU country and so banked customers in it are per se low ris=
k=E2=80=99, =E2=80=98lots of people buy real estate in London and we couldn=
=E2=80=99t possibly inquire about all of them=E2=80=99, and things which we=
 have previously turned a blind eye to, and we will probably lie about havi=
ng turned a blind eye to that, and you will, too, if you know what is good =
for you.=E2=80=9DThat is, the SWIFT cutoff is not really about SWIFT; it is=
 about communicating to international banks that they should enact their ow=
n sanctions on Russia=C2=A0to avoid trouble with their authorities.[3] They=
 should cut off the sorts of Russian transactions that the authorities woul=
d want them to cut off. What those sorts of transactions are is not spelled=
 out, but the banks are smart and can probably figure it out.I think that t=
his is probably the correct model for most other sanctions on Russia, too. =
For an international bank or investor or company, determining that Russia i=
s =E2=80=9Cuninvestable=E2=80=9D is not a matter of parsing the text of the=
 sanctions declarations and deciding that there is no legal way to own Russ=
ian assets; it is a matter of observing the overall mood of those declarati=
ons and saying =E2=80=9Chey this seems like a huge headache and not=C2=A0wo=
rth our while.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0But, as I said at the end of the previous sect=
ion, there is another side to that trade. Big banks with lots of regulatory=
 entanglements are going to say =E2=80=9Cthis is not worth our while,=E2=80=
=9D which will have the effect of making various Russian transactions diffi=
cult even if they are not technically sanctioned. Other firms will care les=
s about reputational risk and good relations with regulators, and will spec=
ialize in reading the rules closely,=C2=A0figuring out exactly what they=E2=
=80=99re allowed to do, and doing it. Probably there is money to be made wi=
th that approach.

Here=E2=80=99s another weird one. Russia has international debt held by int=
ernational investors. It is now much more likely to default on that debt =
=E2=80=94 perhaps because it will have limited access to hard currency, bec=
ause it decides it doesn=E2=80=99t want to pay that currency to internation=
al investors,=C2=A0because sanctions block it from using international rail=
s to make payments, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0than it was two weeks ago. It is no=
t a foregone conclusion or anything, and=C2=A0Russian corporate issuers see=
m to be continuing to make payments on foreign bonds, but the market sugges=
ts =E2=80=9Ca 65% chance of default within five years and 40% within one ye=
ar=E2=80=9D for Russian foreign-currency government bonds.Some internationa=
l investors own credit default swaps to hedge against the risk of Russian d=
efault. If Russia defaults, the CDS contracts are supposed to make good the=
 investors=E2=80=99 losses. If you own $100 of Russian bonds and $100 of CD=
S, and Russia defaults and the bonds only pay you back=C2=A0$30, then you a=
re supposed to get back $70 on your CDS.There are relatively clean ways for=
 defaults like this to happen. A small country can run out of money, call u=
p its bondholders and say =E2=80=9Cwe=E2=80=99ll give you new bonds worth $=
30 for your old $100 bonds.=E2=80=9D The bondholders can say =E2=80=9Cehh t=
hat=E2=80=99s the best we're gonna do=E2=80=9D and take the deal. They=E2=
=80=99ll get $30 of new stuff. CDS will pay out $70.If Russia defaults it w=
ill definitely not happen in this clean way. I don=E2=80=99t know how it wi=
ll happen, though some variant on =E2=80=9CRussia stops making payments on =
bonds and does nothing else=E2=80=9D seems pretty likely to me. It will not=
 do a big negotiated restructuring of the bonds in which it issues new, les=
s valuable bonds to international investors, because (1) it won=E2=80=99t w=
ant to and (2) sanctions might make it impossible for the international inv=
estors to negotiate that deal anyway. Any default will not be resolved clea=
nly but will leave defaulted bonds in limbo for a while.So, fine. Russia mi=
ght stop paying interest on its bonds. They would then be in default and CD=
S would be triggered. But how much would it pay? In theory, $100 of CDS wou=
ld pay $100 minus whatever a $100 Russian bond is worth. In practice, what =
would a Russian bond be worth? Roughly the answer is some market price refl=
ecting people=E2=80=99s expectation of some eventual settlement, but that r=
equires there to be a market. (More technically the answer is that there is=
 an auction of the debt to determine the CDS settlement price, but that req=
uires there to be an auction.)=C2=A0If you can=E2=80=99t trade Russian bond=
s on the secondary market =E2=80=94=C2=A0because sanctions make it illegal,=
 or because the general halo of sanctions makes it=C2=A0feel=C2=A0illegal =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0then, uh:International sanctions placed on the country in re=
sponse to President Vladimir Putin=E2=80=99s invasion of Ukraine may both t=
rigger credit-default swaps and also prevent the underlying bonds from bein=
g used for settlement, according to strategists and investors at Citigroup =
Inc., CreditSights Inc. and Vanguard Asset Management. =E2=80=A6Citi compar=
ed Russia=E2=80=99s potential settlement problems to Novo Banco SA, which w=
as left without deliverable bonds in 2016 when the Bank of Portugal decided=
 to transfer the notes to a bad bank. That event suggested that rules intro=
duced two years earlier to fix flaws still weren=E2=80=99t enough to give b=
ondholders full protection when governments or regulators intervene.A poten=
tial ban on secondary trading of Russian sovereign bonds could create an or=
phaning event similar to Novo Banco=E2=80=99s, whereby the derivatives beco=
me worthless because there=E2=80=99s no underlying debt to insure, Citi sai=
d.=C2=A0=E2=80=9CIt doesn=E2=80=99t seem like an imminent risk yet, but inv=
estors have to be aware of the risk,=E2=80=9D the strategists said. =E2=80=
=9CIn a trigger situation, the credit derivatives determination committee w=
ill have to define how universal is the inability to transfer bonds at sett=
lement.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0The technical dynamics of the situation are, I think,=
 even stranger than that makes it sound. Basically when CDS is triggered, t=
here is an auction for the bonds; when the bonds trade in a liquid market a=
nd there are many more of them than there are CDS contracts, that should le=
ad to a fair price for the bonds, and that price is used to calculate CDS s=
ettlement.[4] When it is not clear that you can buy=C2=A0or=C2=A0sell the b=
onds, it is not obvious what the clearing price of the auction would be. If=
 one seller decides it can sell and no one can buy, is the price zero? If o=
ne buyer decides it can buy and no one can sell, is the price $100?

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong> <em>Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.</em></p><table class=3D"header" =
width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=
-table-lspace: 0pt; mso-table-rspace: 0pt;">
<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Uninvestable</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sometimes a financial asset trades for=
 $100 one day and $5 the next. If you own it, you have a 95% mark-to-market=
 loss. You might, for various reasons, have to sell it quickly at a loss. P=
erhaps you are a mutual fund and investors in your fund all want to redeem =
because you lost 95% of their money, so you are forced to sell the asset to=
 raise cash. Or perhaps you are a levered investor:=C2=A0a hedge fund, or j=
ust a person with a margin loan. You paid for the asset with $50 of your ow=
n money and $50 borrowed from the bank, and now it is worth $5. The bank ca=
lls you up and says =E2=80=9Chey=C2=A0you borrowed $50 against something no=
w worth $5, so would you mind paying us back $45?=E2=80=9D[1] And you polit=
ely decline, because (1) why give the bank $45 to keep something worth $5 a=
nd (2) given the circumstances you will probably have a hard time raising t=
he $45. So the bank=C2=A0seizes the asset, sells it for $5, keeps the $5=C2=
=A0and has a $45 loss on its loan.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is =
bad, but it is very much stuff that everyone understands and prepares for. =
You might get forced out of a trade at a loss, the bank might lose money on=
 margin loans, it=E2=80=99s normal stuff. The bigger, stranger problem is w=
hen a financial asset trades at $100 one day and=C2=A0<em>doesn=E2=80=99t t=
rade=C2=A0</em>the next. One day willing sellers sell it to willing buyers =
for $100; the next day lots of people are desperate to sell but no one can =
buy. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99ll sell it for $5, we don=E2=80=99t care, we just =
want out,=E2=80=9D the sellers say, but the buyers can=E2=80=99t buy. Maybe=
 the stock exchange has closed. Maybe it has become illegal to transact in =
the asset. Perhaps there is a little trading =E2=80=94=C2=A0perhaps some bu=
yers in some venue can buy the asset at distressed prices even as most buye=
rs can=E2=80=99t =E2=80=94=C2=A0but not enough for all the sellers to sell.=
 =E2=80=9CThis asset was worth $100 yesterday and is worth $5 today,=E2=80=
=9D you can say, fine, but the problem is different. When investors come to=
 your fund looking to redeem, you can=E2=80=99t just get $5 in cash to hand=
 back to them, because you can=E2=80=99t sell. When the bank forecloses on =
the margin loan, it can=E2=80=99t sell to get some cash back; it is stuck h=
olding the thing indefinitely.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDIvbXNjaS1wdWxscy1ydXN=
zaWFuLWVxdWl0aWVzLW91dC1vZi1pdHMta2V5LWVtZXJnaW5nLW1hcmtldC1pbmRleD9jbXBpZD=
1CQkQwMzAzMjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xld=
HRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMDMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524=
a319a23cB5168f7b6" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D=
"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-=
bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid=
; background-color: #ccc;">don=E2=80=99t know</a>!</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">MSCI Inc. and FTSE Russell are cutting Russian equitie=
s from widely-tracked indexes, while the London Stock Exchange suspends doz=
ens of Russian depositary receipts from trading, isolating the stocks from =
a large segment of the investment-fund industry.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16p=
x 0;">An overwhelming majority of market participants see the Russian marke=
t as =E2=80=9Cuninvestable=E2=80=9D and its securities will be removed from=
 emerging markets indexes effective March 9, MSCI said. FTSE Russell will d=
elete Russia constituents listed on the Moscow Exchange at a zero value on =
March 7. Meanwhile, trading in 28 depositary receipts for Russian companies=
 have been suspended on the LSE, Chief Executive Officer David Schwimmer sa=
id in an interview with Bloomberg Television on Thursday. =E2=80=A6</p><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe can=E2=80=99t sell our Russian stocks,=
=E2=80=9D said Russel Chesler, head of investments and capital markets at f=
und manager VanEck Associates Corp. in Sydney. =C2=A0=E2=80=9CEven last wee=
k our brokers wouldn=E2=80=99t sell them when the markets were open, and th=
is will just deteriorate things further for investors.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The London Stock Exchange=E2=80=99s move to susp=
end Russian depositary receipts is =E2=80=9Cin connection with events in Uk=
raine, in light of market conditions, and in order to maintain orderly mark=
ets,=E2=80=9D LSE said in a statement. It also noted that less than 1% of i=
ts total income comes from operations in Russia and Ukraine.</p></blockquot=
e><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On the one hand, if big Russian stocks have =
<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aH=
R0cHM6Ly9maW5hbmNlLnlhaG9vLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL25ld3MvbG9uZG9uLXNjcmFwcy10cmFka=
W5nLXJ1c3NpYW4tc2hhcmVzLTEyMzk1MTg4Mi5odG1s/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB39d6b1=
c2" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">lost 95% to 99% of their value</a>, presumably levered global =
investors are <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2689=
9391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDI=
tMjQvdWJzLXRyaWdnZXJzLW1hcmdpbi1jYWxscy1hcy1pdC1jdXRzLXJ1c3NpYS1ib25kLXZhbH=
Vlcy10by16ZXJvP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDMyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmd=
XRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlz=
dHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfaaf2bbf" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">getting margin calls</a>=
. On the other hand, if you are a levered global investor, you can=E2=80=99=
t sell your Russian stocks in Russia (the exchange is closed and brokers ha=
ve been told not to handle sales for international investors) or abroad (th=
e LSE has suspended trading in the depository receipts), and your bank prob=
ably can=E2=80=99t either. The whole system is a bit frozen.</p><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Or: On the one hand, if you are an emerging-markets index=
 fund and big Russian stocks have been removed from your index for being =
=E2=80=9Cuninvestable,=E2=80=9D presumably you have to go sell those stocks=
 to continue tracking the index. On the other hand, <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZ=
XJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDMvd2FsbC1zdHJlZXQtZnVuZC1naWFudHMt=
ZmFjZS1uZXctaGVhZGFjaGUtYXMtaW5kZXhlcy1kdW1wLXJ1c3NpYT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzAzMjJ=
fTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdG=
VybT0yMjAzMDMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBfc69=
61b3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;"> you can=E2=80=99t</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16=
px 0;">Now, many passive vehicles can=E2=80=99t actually sell to comply wit=
h the index changes, handle investors exiting, or both.=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CFor ETFs such as broad global ETFs or even br=
oad EM ETFs where the Russia holding is limited, the ETF I guess will conti=
nue to hold the Russian assets, tracking a benchmark where these are remove=
d,=E2=80=9D said Matthew Brennan, head of investment management at AJ Bell.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The idea is that the Russian securit=
ies would continue to be held even if valued at zero. This could cause trac=
king error -- when a fund=E2=80=99s actual performance diverges from that o=
f the benchmark it follows -- but also has potential upside should the asse=
ts ever recover.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or here is <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9hLWNsb3NlZC1ydXNzaWEtbWFya2V0LWFmdGVyLWl=
udmFzaW9uLWNoYWxsZW5nZXMtdS1zLWZ1bmQtbWFuYWdlcnMtMTE2NDYzMDM0MDE/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cB19cddd09" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">a weird technical point</a>:</p><blockquot=
e><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some U.S. money managers with Russian securi=
ties held in funds that focus on emerging markets are worried that their in=
vestors will pull money over concern about their Russian exposure. If they =
can=E2=80=99t sell Russian shares to meet the demand, funds could be forced=
 to deplete cash on hand or sell other assets.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">That could deliver a double whammy for emerging-market managers: a redu=
ction in the share of their funds in liquid assets that coincides with an i=
ncrease in the weightings of their Russian holdings.</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">Some firms are thinking about asking the Securities and Exchange =
Commission for relief from a cap on the proportion of illiquid securities t=
hat funds can hold=E2=80=94currently 15%=E2=80=94and from rules governing r=
edemptions, people familiar with the matter said. An SEC spokesman declined=
 to comment.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a mutu=
al fund manager you are in the business of holding mainly liquid publicly t=
raded securities. This is important, because you are also in the business o=
f letting your investors withdraw their money on a daily basis, so if they =
want their money back, you have to sell liquid securities to give it to the=
m. But when some of your liquid publicly traded securities become illiquid =
and untraded, then (1) that=E2=80=99s bad and (2) you=E2=80=99re going to h=
ave to sell your=C2=A0<em>other</em>, still liquid securities to meet redem=
ptions, making your remaining fund both more illiquid and more concentrated=
 in the Russian stocks that have now been kicked out of the index.</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I=E2=80=99m not sure any of this=C2=A0is exactly in=
surmountable: There will probably be=C2=A0some buyers, and some ways to buy=
, so it might=C2=A0eventually get worked out. Awkwardly those buyers will (=
1) provide essential and difficult-to-find liquidity to the global financia=
l system in its hour of need, (2) expect to get paid well for it, (3) buy R=
ussian assets that are under at least an odor of sanctions, and (4) buy the=
m at enormous discounts to their expected cash flows. It might go poorly fo=
r them =E2=80=94=C2=A0Russian assets could always go down <a href=3D"https:=
//link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly90d2l0dGV=
yLmNvbS90aGVzdGFsd2FydC9zdGF0dXMvODc2ODMzOTI5NzUxMzUxMjk2/607f07ceef4b8524a=
319a23cB7c18d904" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"=
_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid;=
 background-color: #ccc;">another 99%</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it is=C2=A0<em=
>in expectation=C2=A0</em>probably a lucrative time not to care about reput=
ation or public opinion.[2] If everyone is selling for noneconomic reasons,=
 buying is more likely to be=C2=A0economically appealing. If you can find a=
 way to do it.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-sponsored-content" width=3D"au=
to" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0">
<tr>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">SWIFT</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last week, one of the most discussed f=
orms of <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.2=
76607/aHR0cHM6Ly9ob21lLnRyZWFzdXJ5Lmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2VzL2p5MDYwO=
A/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf83fd3f5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"it=
emscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !=
important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-b=
ottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">sanction on Russia</a> for its=
 invasion of Ukraine was the idea of cutting Russian banks off of the SWIFT=
 interbank messaging system. This was widely considered to be a very big de=
al because=C2=A0SWIFT is how banks mainly send payment instructions, and wi=
thout access to SWIFT it will be somewhere between administratively annoyin=
g and almost impossible=C2=A0for Russian banks to interact with the global =
financial system. And this week it <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbu=
siness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9saXZlY292ZXJhZ2=
UvcnVzc2lhLXVrcmFpbmUtbGF0ZXN0LW5ld3MtMjAyMi0wMy0wMS9jYXJkL3dlc3Qtb3JkZXJzL=
XNldmVuLXJ1c3NpYW4tYmFua3Mtb2ZmLXN3aWZ0LWJ1dC1sZWF2ZXMtb3RoZXJzLW9uLU9NdjlU=
Q3NaTVFxbHplOWRRUVJt/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6b806e76" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">sort of hap=
pened</a>, on a smallish scale and with lot of carve-outs.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">I think the best and deepest explanation of the SWIFT situa=
tion is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.2=
76607/aHR0cHM6Ly9iYW0ua2FsenVtZXVzLmNvbS9hcmNoaXZlL21vdmluZy1tb25leS1pbnRlc=
m5hdGlvbmFsbHkv/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4048c48a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=
temscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">this post from p=
ayments expert Patrick McKenzie</a>. It includes a discussion of the mechan=
ics of SWIFT and cross-border payments, but that is not really the emphasis=
. Instead the emphasis is on symbolism: Partially cutting some Russian bank=
s off of the SWIFT system =E2=80=9Cdoes not substantially control who is al=
lowed to send wires,=E2=80=9D but it does send a certain sort of message to=
 the banks who interact with those Russian banks. McKenzie:</p><blockquote>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think the best articulation of the strategy =
is =E2=80=9CWe are attempting to convey enormous displeasure while sanction=
ing some banks which are believed to be close to politically exposed Russia=
ns, while not making it impossible for Russian firms generally to transact =
internationally nor sparking a humanitarian crisis either inside or outside=
 of Russia.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Many comment=
ators confuse the actual effects of severing particular banks from SWIFT wi=
th what they perceive as the policy goal motivating it. More important than=
 either is, in my opinion, what it communicates about commander=E2=80=99s i=
ntent to the policy arms who are responsible for enforcing it.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Specifically, it communicates that Something Has Chang=
ed and that Russian institutional money, specifically =E2=80=9Coligarch=E2=
=80=9D money, is now tainted, and not in the benignly ignored fashion it ha=
s been for most of the last few decades.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Re=
ad: =E2=80=9CWe will with absolute certainty hand out billions of dollars o=
f fines stochastically over the next ten years. You can minimize how many h=
it your institution by successfully intuiting who is on the Bad Risks list.=
 We will be sharply less tolerant of =E2=80=98Cyprus is an EU country and s=
o banked customers in it are per se low risk=E2=80=99, =E2=80=98lots of peo=
ple buy real estate in London and we couldn=E2=80=99t possibly inquire abou=
t all of them=E2=80=99, and things which we have previously turned a blind =
eye to, and we will probably lie about having turned a blind eye to that, a=
nd you will, too, if you know what is good for you.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquot=
e><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is, the SWIFT cutoff is not really abou=
t SWIFT; it is about communicating to international banks that they should =
<em>enact their own sanctions on Russia</em>=C2=A0to avoid trouble with the=
ir authorities.[3] They should cut off the sorts of Russian transactions th=
at the authorities would want them to cut off. What those sorts of transact=
ions are is not spelled out, but the banks are smart and can probably figur=
e it out.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I think that this is probably the=
 correct model for most other sanctions on Russia, too. For an internationa=
l bank or investor or company, determining that Russia is =E2=80=9Cuninvest=
able=E2=80=9D is not a matter of parsing the text of the sanctions declarat=
ions and deciding that there is no legal way to own Russian assets; it is a=
 matter of observing the overall mood of those declarations and saying =E2=
=80=9Chey this seems like a huge headache and not=C2=A0worth our while.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But, as I said at the end of t=
he previous section, there is another side to that trade. Big banks with lo=
ts of regulatory entanglements are going to say =E2=80=9Cthis is not worth =
our while,=E2=80=9D which will have the effect of making various Russian tr=
ansactions difficult even if they are not technically sanctioned. Other fir=
ms will care less about reputational risk and good relations with regulator=
s, and will specialize in reading the rules closely,=C2=A0figuring out exac=
tly what they=E2=80=99re allowed to do, and doing it. Probably there is mon=
ey to be made with that approach.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-flex-ad" bo=
rder=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" align=3D"center">

5px;">CDS</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s another weird one. Russ=
ia has international debt held by international investors. It is now much m=
ore likely to default on that debt =E2=80=94 perhaps because it will have l=
imited access to hard currency, because it decides it doesn=E2=80=99t want =
to pay that currency to international investors,=C2=A0because sanctions blo=
ck it from using international rails to make payments, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
than it was two weeks ago. It is not a foregone conclusion or anything, and=
=C2=A0Russian <em>corporate</em> issuers seem to be <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZ=
XJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDMvYS1ydXNzaWFuLWdyb3VwLXMtNS1taWxs=
aW9uLWNvdXBvbi1pcy1zdWRkZW5seS1hLW1hcmtldC1tb21lbnQ_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMwMzIyX01=
PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm=
09MjIwMzAzJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB29d36ff=
d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">continuing to make payments</a> on foreign bonds, but <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDIvcnVzc2lhLXNhbmN0=
aW9ucy1wdXQtNDEtYmlsbGlvbi1vZi1kZWZhdWx0LWluc3VyYW5jZS1hdC1yaXNrP2NtcGlkPUJ=

blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;">the market suggests</a> =E2=80=9Ca 65% chance of d=
efault within five years and 40% within one year=E2=80=9D for Russian forei=
gn-currency government bonds.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Some internat=
ional investors own credit default swaps to hedge against the risk of Russi=
an default. If Russia defaults, the CDS contracts are supposed to make good=
 the investors=E2=80=99 losses. If you own $100 of Russian bonds and $100 o=
f CDS, and Russia defaults and the bonds only pay you back=C2=A0$30, then y=
ou are supposed to get back $70 on your CDS.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;=
">There are relatively clean ways for defaults like this to happen. A small=
 country can run out of money, call up its bondholders and say =E2=80=9Cwe=
=E2=80=99ll give you new bonds worth $30 for your old $100 bonds.=E2=80=9D =
The bondholders can say =E2=80=9Cehh that=E2=80=99s the best we're gonna do=
=E2=80=9D and take the deal. They=E2=80=99ll get $30 of new stuff. CDS will=
 pay out $70.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If Russia defaults it will de=
finitely not happen in this clean way. I don=E2=80=99t know how it will hap=
pen, though some variant on =E2=80=9CRussia stops making payments on bonds =
and does nothing else=E2=80=9D seems pretty likely to me. It will not do a =
big negotiated restructuring of the bonds in which it issues new, less valu=
able bonds to international investors, because (1) it won=E2=80=99t want to=
 and (2) sanctions might make it impossible for the international investors=
 to negotiate that deal anyway. Any default will not be resolved cleanly bu=
t will leave defaulted bonds in limbo for a while.</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">So, fine. Russia might stop paying interest on its bonds. They woul=
d then be in default and CDS would be triggered. But how much would it pay?=
 In theory, $100 of CDS would pay $100 minus whatever a $100 Russian bond i=
s worth. In practice, what would a Russian bond be worth? Roughly the answe=
r is some market price reflecting people=E2=80=99s expectation of some even=
tual settlement, but that requires there to be a market. (More technically =
the answer is that there is an auction of the debt to determine the CDS set=
tlement price, but that requires there to be an auction.)=C2=A0If you can=
=E2=80=99t trade Russian bonds on the secondary market =E2=80=94=C2=A0becau=
se sanctions make it illegal, or because the general halo of sanctions make=
s it=C2=A0<em>feel=C2=A0</em>illegal =E2=80=94=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZ=
XJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDIvcnVzc2lhLXNhbmN0aW9ucy1wdXQtNDEt=
YmlsbGlvbi1vZi1kZWZhdWx0LWluc3VyYW5jZS1hdC1yaXNrP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDMyMl9NT05=
FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPT=
IyMDMwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC338c8106"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;">then, uh</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Internat=
ional sanctions placed on the country in response to President Vladimir Put=
in=E2=80=99s invasion of Ukraine may both trigger credit-default swaps and =
also prevent the underlying bonds from being used for settlement, according=
 to strategists and investors at Citigroup Inc., CreditSights Inc. and Vang=
uard Asset Management. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Citi compa=
red Russia=E2=80=99s potential settlement problems to Novo Banco SA, which =
was left without deliverable bonds in 2016 when the Bank of Portugal decide=
d to transfer the notes to a bad bank. That event suggested that rules intr=
oduced two years earlier to fix flaws still weren=E2=80=99t enough to give =
bondholders full protection when governments or regulators intervene.</p><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A potential ban on secondary trading of Russian =
sovereign bonds could create an orphaning event similar to Novo Banco=E2=80=
=99s, whereby the derivatives become worthless because there=E2=80=99s no u=
nderlying debt to insure, Citi said.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
=E2=80=9CIt doesn=E2=80=99t seem like an imminent risk yet, but investors h=
ave to be aware of the risk,=E2=80=9D the strategists said. =E2=80=9CIn a t=
rigger situation, the credit derivatives determination committee will have =
to define how universal is the inability to transfer bonds at settlement.=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The technical =
dynamics of the situation are, I think, even stranger than that makes it so=
und. Basically when CDS is triggered, there is an auction for the bonds; wh=
en the bonds trade in a liquid market and there are many more of them than =
there are CDS contracts, that should lead to a fair price for the bonds, an=
d that price is used to calculate CDS settlement.[4] When it is not clear t=
hat you can buy=C2=A0<em>or=C2=A0</em>sell the bonds, it is not obvious wha=
t the clearing price of the auction would be. If one seller decides it can =
sell and no one can buy, is the price zero? If one buyer decides it can buy=
 and no one can sell, is the price $100?</p><table class=3D"header" width=
=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0">
<tr>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Oil and gold</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The international sanctions regime has=
 been fairly careful to exempt Russian energy production from sanctions, de=
spite many calls to sanction energy specifically. <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/26899391.276607/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDMvcnVzc2lhLXMtdG94aWMtb2lsLXNlbmRzLX=
